narrative:
Hello! I understand that receiving a rejection for your university application can be disheartening, and I'm here to help you understand why this decision was made and what you can do moving forward. The model used to evaluate your application predicted a 44% probability that you might not pass the bar exam on your first attempt. This prediction was based on several factors from your academic and personal background.

Let's start with your academic performance. Your grades in Year 1 and Year 3 placed you in the second decile at your school, which is lower than the average decile of students who typically pass the bar exam. This was one of the most influential factors in the model's prediction. Additionally, your LSAT score was below the average score of students who pass, which also contributed to the prediction that you might struggle with the bar exam.

Your family income was considered as well, and while it was in the middle bracket, it was slightly below the average income level of students who tend to pass. This factor, along with your racial background as Hispanic, played a role in the prediction, although they were not as influential as your academic performance and LSAT score.

On a positive note, your undergraduate GPA was slightly above average, which worked in your favor, suggesting that you have a strong academic foundation. Your gender and full-time status had minimal impact on the prediction, with your full-time status slightly pushing the prediction towards passing.

Given this information, you might consider focusing on improving your LSAT score, as it is a critical component of the application process and can significantly influence your chances of success. Additionally, seeking academic support to enhance your performance in key areas could also be beneficial. Remember, this prediction is not a definitive outcome but rather an assessment based on current data. With targeted efforts, you can improve your chances of passing the bar exam and achieving your academic goals.
